|website=www.doc.govt.nz |publisher=Department of Conservation |language=en}} ''{{lang|mi|makomako}}'' and ''{{lang|mi|kopara}}''.{{sfn|Best|1977|p=321}} The male bellbird is known as ''{{lang|mi|kēkerematua}}'' or ''{{lang|mi|kerekerematātu}}''.{{Cite journal |title=He manu |url= https://ngaitahu.iwi.nz/opportunities-and-resources/publications/te-karaka/ahakoa-he-iti-he-pounamu-2/ |journal=Te Karaka |date=2012 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20240620000755/https://ngaitahu.iwi.nz/opportunities-and-resources/publications/te-karaka/ahakoa-he-iti-he-pounamu-2/ |archive-date=20 June 2024 |access-date=24 August 2024 |via=ngaitahu.iwi.nz |publisher=Te Rūnanga o Ngāi Tahu |language=en-NZ |url-status=live }} The English common name "bellbird" originates from the imaginative similarity of one of its notes to the distant ringing of a bell.{{sfn|Buller|1873|p=89}} The subspecies epithet ''oneho'' is named after a hill (216 m) on Aorangi Island (one of the [[Poor Knights Islands]]). The hill was named after Oneho, the wife of [[Tatua]], the last chief of the Ngatitoki [[hapū]] that inhabited the island until the 1820s. ''Obscurus'', another subspecies epithet, comes from Latin ''{{lang|la|obscurus}}'' ("dark", "dusky").{{sfn|Jobling|2010|p=278}} === Classification === [[File:Kari Mako (Korimako) Mahurangi N.Z. by Charles Heaphy.jpg|thumb|Illustration of the bellbird by [[Charles Heaphy]], 1853]] The bellbird was originally placed into the genus ''[[Certhia]].'' In 1840, [[George Robert Gray]] established a genus ''[[Anthornis]]'', where he moved the bellbird.{{Cite book |last=Gray |first=George Robert |author-link=George Robert Gray |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/bibliography/13777 |title=A list of the genera of birds, with an indication of the typical species of each genus, compiled from various sources |date=1840 |publisher=Richard and John Taylor |location=[London] |page=[https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/13668909#page/29/mode/1up 15] |doi=10.5962/bhl.title.13777}} The bellbird is the [[type species]] of the genus. This classification has been widely accepted since then.{{Cite web |year=2022 |title=Checklist of the Birds of New Zealand |url=https://www.birdsnz.org.nz/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/checklist-2022.pdf |publisher=Ornithological Society of New Zealand |place=Wellington |pages=203–204 |edition=5th}} For a long time, the New Zealand bellbird was the sole representative of the genus ''Anthornis''. The [[Chatham Islands bellbird]] (''A. melanocephala''), which became [[extinct]] in the early 20th century, was formerly classified as a [[subspecies]] of the New Zealand bellbird, as ''A. m. melanocephala''. The Chatham Islands bellbird is now recognised as a separate species and a second member of the genus. The New Zealand bellbird and the [[tūī]] (''Prosthemadera novaeseelandiae'') are the only representatives of [[honeyeater]]s (Meliphagidae) in New Zealand.{{Cite journal |last1=Craig |first1=John L. |last2=Stewart |first2=Anne M. |last3=Douglas |first3=Murray E. |date=1981 |title=The foraging of New Zealand honeyeaters |url=http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/03014223.1981.10427945 |journal=New Zealand Journal of Zoology |language=en |volume=8 |issue=1 |pages=87–91 |doi=10.1080/03014223.1981.10427945 |issn=0301-4223|url-access=subscription }} The [[Stitchbird|hihi or stitchbird]] (''Notiomystis cincta''), another New Zealand endemic bird, was originally considered to be a honeyeater also, but it is now believed to constitute its own [[Monotypic taxon|monotypic]] family, Notiomystidae.{{Cite journal |last1=Ewen |first1=John G. |last2=Flux |first2=Ian |last3=Ericson |first3=Per G. P. |date=1 July 2006 |title=Systematic affinities of two enigmatic New Zealand passerines of high conservation priority, the hihi or stitchbird ''Notiomystis cincta'' and the kokako ''Callaeas cinerea'' |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1055790306000339 |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=40 |issue=1 |pages=281–284 |doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2006.01.026 |pmid=16527495 |bibcode=2006MolPE..40..281E |issn=1055-7903|url-access=subscription }}{{Cite journal |last1=Driskell |first1=Amy |last2=Christidis |first2=Les |last3=Gill |first3=B. J. |last4=Boles |first4=Walter E. |last5=Barker |first5=F. Keith |last6=Longmore |first6=N. W. |date=28 May 2007 |title=A new endemic family of New Zealand passerine birds: adding heat to a biodiversity hotspot |url=https://www.publish.csiro.au/zo/ZO07007 |journal=Australian Journal of Zoology |language=en |volume=55 |issue=2 |pages=73–78 |doi=10.1071/ZO07007 |issn=1446-5698|url-access=subscription }} A [[Molecular biology|molecular]] study from 2017 identified the Chatham Islands bellbird as the closest relative to the New Zealand bellbird. Both species constitute a [[clade]] to which the tūī is a [[Sister group|sister taxon]]. Other close relatives of the bellbirds and the tūī are the [[Marbled honeyeater|marbled]] and [[plain honeyeater]]s from [[New Guinea]]. Their [[Phylogenetics|phylogenetic]] relationships are shown in the [[cladogram]] below:{{Cite journal |last1=Marki |first1=Petter Z. |last2=Jønsson |first2=Knud A. |last3=Irestedt |first3=Martin |last4=Nguyen |first4=Jacqueline M.T. |last5=Rahbek |first5=Carsten |last6=Fjeldså |first6=Jon |date=2017 |title=Supermatrix phylogeny and biogeography of the Australasian Meliphagides radiation (Aves: Passeriformes) |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S1055790316304444 |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |language=en |volume=107 |pages=516–529 |doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2016.12.021|pmid=28017855 |bibcode=2017MolPE.107..516M |hdl=10852/65203 |hdl-access=free }} {{clade|{{clade |1={{clade |1=[[plain honeyeater]] (''Pycnopygius ixoides'') |2=[[marbled honeyeater]] (''Pycnopygius cinereus'') }} |2={{clade |1={{clade |1=[[tūī]] (''Prosthemadera novaeseelandiae'') |2={{clade |1='''New Zealand bellbird''' (''Anthornis melanura'') |2=†[[Chatham Islands bellbird]] (''Anthornis melanocephala'') }} }} }} }}}} === Subspecies === There are three recognised [[subspecies]] of the New Zealand bellbird with the following distribution: * ''A. melanura melanura'' (Sparrman, 1786) – occurs in [[North Island]], [[South Island]], [[Stewart Island]] and the [[Auckland Islands]] * ''A. melanura oneho'' ([[Robert Falla|Falla]], 1948) – the [[Manawatāwhi / Three Kings Islands]] * ''A. melanura obscura'' (Bartle & Sagar, 1987){{Cite journal |last=Falla |first=R. A. |year=1948 |title=A New ''Anthornis'' from Three Kings Islands |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/289985#page/475/mode/1up |journal=Records of the Auckland Institute and Museum |publisher=Auckland Institute and Museum |volume=3 |page=337 |lccn=42048001}} – the [[Poor Knights Islands]] ==Description== {{Multiple image|total_width = 200 | align = | direction = vertical | background color = | width = | caption_align = | image_style = | image_gap = | header_background = | header_align = | header = | image1 = Male and female bellbirds clinging to a branch (Tiritiri Matangi).jpg | width1 = | alt1 = | thumbtime1 = | caption1 = Female (left) and male (right) | footer_background = | footer_align = | footer = }} Bellbirds are medium-sized honeyeaters, about 17–20 cm in length from the tip of their [[beak]] to the end of their [[tail]]. Females weigh about 25{{nbsp}}g and males 33{{nbsp}}g.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1173}} Males have an olive-green body with a dark purplish sheen on their head. Their belly and flanks are lighter.{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=172}} The uppertail is black-brown with a narrow [[Iridescence|iridescent]] blue-black edge on most feathers. The upperwing [[Covert feather|coverts]] are mostly dark olive-green, with blackish inner webs. The [[Cloaca|vent]] and undertail-coverts are pale yellow to off-white, and the thighs are grey. The underwing is mainly grey with a dark olive leading edge, pale-yellow secondary coverts, and a brown tint to the [[remiges]].{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1174}} There is a yellow patch at the bend of a folded wing.{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=172}} The [[Iris (anatomy)|iris]] of the male is red.{{Cite book |title=Reader's Digest Complete Book of New Zealand Birds |date=2010 |publisher=Reader's Digest |editor-last=Robertson |editor-first=C. J. R. |location=Auckland |page=290}} Females are slightly smaller than males. Their [[plumage]] is duller and much browner than adult males.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1174}} The gloss on the head is bluish, and there is a narrow white stripe starting at the [[gape]] and continuing below the eye. Upperparts are dark olive-brown with dark brown uppertail and olive-edged outer webs. The upperwing is mostly dark olive-brown with dark brown inner webs and olive outer edges. Underparts are mostly olive-brown with a patch of pale yellow feathers on the flanks, less prominent than in adult males, and pale olive to off-white vent and undertail-coverts. The undertail is brownish grey, and the underwing resembles that of an adult male.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1174}} The iris of a female is brown. Bare parts of males and females are the same. The bill is black and slightly curved downwards. Legs are grey-black, claws brown. Similar to other honeyeaters, bellbirds have a brush-like tongue that is used to collect nectar deeply from flowers.{{Cite encyclopedia |last=Sagar |first=P. M. |date=2022 |title=Bellbird / Korimako |encyclopedia= New Zealand Birds Online |url= https://www.nzbirdsonline.org.nz/species/bellbird |access-date=2 May 2026 |language=en }} The tongue is longitudinally grooved, and its tip is divided into four segments. The end of each segment is equipped with fine hair-like structures that form the final brush.{{Cite journal |last=McCann |first=Charles |title=Observations on the tongues of some New Zealand birds |url=https://www.birdsnz.org.nz/publications/observations-on-the-tongues-of-some-new-zealand-birds/ |journal=Notornis |volume=11 |issue=1 |pages=36–45}} In both sexes, the ninth primary feather is notched. Sometimes, the eighth or tenth or all three mentioned primaries are notched too.{{Cite journal |last=Craig |first=John L. |date=1984 |title=Wing noises, wing slots, and aggression in New Zealand honeyeaters (Aves: Meliphagidae) |url= http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/03014223.1984.10423758 |journal=New Zealand Journal of Zoology |volume=11 |issue=2 |pages=195–199 |doi=10.1080/03014223.1984.10423758 |issn=0301-4223}} Both of the subspecies ''oneho'' and ''obscura'' are very similar in appearance to the nominate subspecies described above.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1185}}{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1189}} The male of the subspecies ''oneho'' has more blue sheen on its head, and feathers on some upper parts (nape, forehead, crown, ear coverts, chin, and throat) have a violet sheen.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1185}} The female of the subspecies ''oneho'' differs from the nominate female by having a greener sheen on the head and neck, and a green-blue sheen on the crown, forehead, nape, and ear coverts.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1186}} Compared to mainland birds, males of the subspecies ''oneho'' are relatively larger in proportion to females. The male of the subspecies ''obscura'' has slightly darker plumage than the nominate male, and its sheen on the upper body parts is violet. The female of the subspecies ''obscura'' has slightly paler underparts compared to the nominate female.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1189}} A juvenile male is similar to an adult male but is duller with very little to no iridescence. A juvenile female is even paler and greyer than a juvenile male.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1174}} Unlike adults, juvenile bellbirds do not have their primaries notched. Nestlings are initially born without feathers, but quickly develop a covering of grey [[Down feather|down]]. ==Distribution and habitat== [[File:Juvenile bellbird perched on a twig.jpg|thumb|Juvenile bellbird perched on a twig]] The most widespread subspecies by far is ''melanura'', which can be found throughout [[North Island|North]], [[South Island|South]] and [[Stewart Island]] and the [[Auckland Islands]].{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|pp=404–405}} It is also present on many satellites, like [[Tiritiri Matangi Island|Tiritiri Matangi]],{{Cite web |title=The Sanctuary – Tiritiri Matangi |url=https://www.tiritirimatangi.org.nz/the-sanctuary/ |access-date=19 June 2024 |publisher=Supporters of Tiritiri Matangi Inc. |language=en-UK}} [[Little Barrier Island|Little Barrier]], [[Cuvier Island|Cuvier]] and [[Mayor Island / Tūhua|Mayor Island]], and the [[Mokohinau Islands|Mokohinau]], [[Mercury Islands|Mercury]], [[Hen and Chicken Islands|Hen and Chickens]] and [[Aldermen Islands|Aldermen Islands]].{{sfn|Lee|2005|p=150}} [[Vagrancy (biology)|Vagrants]] have appeared as far south as [[Campbell Island, New Zealand|Campbell Island]]{{Cite journal |last=Scofield |first=R. Paul |date=2006 |title=Rare Birds Committee Report for the year to 31st July 2006 |url=https://www.birdsnz.org.nz/wp-content/uploads/rare-birds-migrated-files/MUpgXHviSN.pdf |journal=Southern Bird |volume=27}} and there is a historical record from the [[Snares Islands]] dating back to 1888.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1175}} Subspecies ''oneho'' only occurs on the [[Manawatāwhi / Three Kings Islands|Three Kings Islands]], and subspecies ''obscura'' only nests on the [[Poor Knights Islands]], but occasionally visits the nearby mainland of eastern Northland. Bellbirds are almost absent north of [[Waikato]]. [[Northland Region|Northland]] can get occasional winter female visitors from nearby offshore islands, and small pocket populations exist in some places north of [[Auckland]], like [[Tāwharanui Peninsula]] and [[Shakespear Regional Park]].{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=404}} It is also scarce in the [[Canterbury Plains]] and [[Central Otago]]. In their native forest habitat, bellbirds can be abundant and are more widely distributed than tūī.{{Cite journal |last1=Brunton |first1=Dianne H. |last2=Evans |first2=Barbara A. |last3=Ji |first3=Weihong |date=2008 |title=Assessing natural dispersal of New Zealand bellbirds using song type and song playbacks |url=https://newzealandecology.org/nzje/2872 |journal=New Zealand Journal of Ecology |volume=32 |issue=2 |pages=147–154 |ref=Brunton2008}} [[File:Anthornis melanura 25470849.jpg|left|thumb|Male bellbird on a [[kōwhai]] tree, [[Banks Peninsula]]]] The bellbird's [[habitat]] encompasses both native and exotic forests and [[scrubland]]s, and it is frequently found in urban areas such as [[park]]s and gardens,{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=172}} especially if there is a nearby patch of native [[The bush#New Zealand|bush]]. It is found from lowland areas at sea level up to about 1200 metres, including [[subalpine]] regions above the [[tree line]]. It particularly thrives in densely vegetated areas with a mix of [[Podocarpus|podocarp]]-hardwood lowland forests with diverse vegetation. It is typically found in forests dominated by [[Metrosideros robusta|northern rātā]] (''Metrosideros robusta''), [[Quintinia acutifolia|tawheowheo]] (''Quintinia serrata''), [[Pterophylla racemosa|kāmahi]] (''Weinmannia racemosa''), as well as mixed southern beech forests (''[[Nothofagus]]'' species) or coastal broadleaf forests, such as [[pōhutukawa]] (''Metrosideros excelsa''). Additionally, it can be found in exotic forests such as [[eucalyptus]], [[acacia]], and even [[pine]] and [[willow]] forests. Occasionally, it ventures into farmland, including [[orchard]]s.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|pp=1174–1175}} On predator-free islands, bellbirds typically reach higher [[Population density|population densities]] than on the mainland.{{Cite journal |last1=Anderson |first1=S.H. |last2=Craig |first2=J.L. |date=2003 |title=Breeding biology of bellbirds (''Anthornis melanura'') on Tiritiri Matangi Island |url=https://www.birdsnz.org.nz/publications/breeding-biology-of-bellbirds-anthornis-melanura-on-tiritiri-matangi-island/ |journal=Notornis |volume=50 |issue=2 |pages=75–82 |ref=Anderson2003}} On the Poor Knights Islands, subspecies ''oneho'' reaches densities of 71 birds per hectare.{{Cite journal |last=Sagar |first=P. M. |date=1985 |title=Breeding of the bellbird on the Poor Knights Islands, New Zealand |url=http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/03014223.1985.10428314 |journal=New Zealand Journal of Zoology |volume=12 |issue=4 |pages=643–648 |doi=10.1080/03014223.1985.10428314 |issn=0301-4223|url-access=subscription }} However, this figure was derived from a study area of only 0.45 hectares, and therefore may not accurately reflect the entire 66-hectare island.{{Cite journal |last1=Sagar |first1=P. M. |last2=Scofield |first2=R. P. |date=2006 |title=Survival, density and population composition of bellbirds (''Anthornis melanura'') on the Poor Knights Islands, New Zealand |url= http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/03014223.2006.9518454 |journal=New Zealand Journal of Zoology |volume=33 |issue=4 |pages=249–257 |doi=10.1080/03014223.2006.9518454 |issn=0301-4223|url-access=subscription }} On the predator-free Great Island in the Three Kings Islands, the density of bellbirds was estimated to be 16 birds per hectare{{Cite journal |last1=Turbott |first1=E. G. |author1-link=Graham Turbott |last2=Bull |first2=P. C. |date=1954 |title=A Bird Census and Some Recent Observations on Birds on Great Island, Three Kings Group |journal=Records of the Auckland Institute and Museum |volume=4 |issue=4 |pages=245–262 |jstor=42906071 |issn=0067-0464}} and on Tiritiri Matangi Island 2.13 pairs per hectare. ==Behaviour and ecology== During the breeding season, bellbirds mostly remain within their [[Territory (animal)|territories]] but may leave to feed at nearby food sources. Outside of this period, they are solitary and nomadic.{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=406}} The bellbird's flight is noisy and direct.{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=172}} While some populations are residential, some may [[Bird migration|migrate]] locally. Seasonal migrations were recorded between bush in the winter and summer coastal forest, or between river beds and urban areas.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1177}} They are also capable of moving between offshore islands and the mainland. Bellbirds from [[Tawharanui]] are believed to have flown from [[Little Barrier Island]], which is 23 km away across the open sea.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1180}} Main natural [[Predation|predators]] of bellbirds are the [[swamp harrier]] (''Circus approximans'') and the [[New Zealand falcon]] (''Falco novaeseelandiae''). In the Auckland Islands, bellbirds were the most commonly eaten prey of falcons.{{Cite journal |last1=Hyde |first1=N.H.S. |last2=Worthy |first2=T.H. |date=2010 |title=The diet of New Zealand falcons (''Falco novaeseelandiae'') on the Auckland Islands, New Zealand |url=https://www.birdsnz.org.nz/publications/the-diet-of-new-zealand-falcons-falco-novaeseelandiae-on-the-auckland-islands-new-zealand/ |journal=Notornis |volume=57 |issue=1 |pages=19–26}} Bellbirds can also be attacked by [[Australian magpie]] (''Gymnorhina tibicen'').{{Cite journal |last1=Morgan |first1=D. |last2=Waas |first2=J. R. |last3=Innes |first3=J. |date=2005 |title=Magpie interactions with other birds in New Zealand: results from a literature review and public survey |url=https://www.birdsnz.org.nz/publications/magpie-interactions-with-other-birds-in-new-zealand-results-from-a-literature-review-and-public-survey/ |journal=Notornis |volume=52 |issue=2 |pages=61–74}} ===Breeding=== [[File:NZ Bellbird (Korimako) (51648711655).jpg|thumb|Bellbird (male) feeding on [[Melianthus major|honey bush]], an exotic plant. The pollen on its forehead will help pollinate other flowers.|214x214px]] Bellbirds are [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]] and establish long-term bonds. They keep the same territory each year.{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=405}} On Poor Knight Islands, adult males were defending their territories year-round. During the breeding season, a female shared a male's territory and helped defend it by chasing away other bellbirds. After breeding, she became non-territorial and, especially in autumn, joined flocks of juveniles and immature birds. During breeding, bellbirds vigorously defend their territories, with females willing to physically attack intruders. Sometimes, females attempt to distract approaching predators by falling to the ground and flapping away through dense undergrowth. [[File:Bellbird_feeding_on_mountain_beech_honeydew.jpg|alt=Image of New Zealand Bellbird feeding on honeydew on the trunk of a mountain beech tree. Craigieburn Forest.|right|thumb|Bellbird (male) feeding on [[Honeydew (secretion)|honeydew]] on the trunk of a [[Nothofagus|mountain beech]] tree, [[Craigieburn Forest Park|Craigieburn Forest]]]] Courtship occurs in August and September, during which a pair of bellbirds can be seen chasing each other or singing together. Male courtship behaviour includes hovering or slowly ascending with his body in a vertical position, producing a whirring sound with his wings. Courtship feeding sometimes occurs, during which the male feeds the female.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1182}} Courtship behaviour intensifies as they get closer to nest building. The female constructs a cup-shaped [[Bird nest|nest]] from twigs, moss and fibres,{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=405}} lining it with fine grasses, wool and feathers.{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=405}} While the nest is usually situated in a tree, on some islands it may be located in a rock or trunk cavity.{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=405}} On a tree, it could be placed from ground level to about 15 m high. On Tiritiri Matangi, most nests were placed under the hanging [[frond]]s of [[tree fern]]s. Nest building takes about 3–7 days. Mainland bellbirds generally lay eggs between September and January, typically raising two [[Broodiness|broods]]. In contrast, bellbirds from the Poor Knights Islands lay eggs from late September to late November and usually have only one brood.{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=405}} Females lay 3–4 eggs at daily intervals.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1184}} The eggs measure approximately 23×16 mm and are pinkish with red-brown spots.{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=405}} Only females [[Egg incubation|incubate]] the eggs, while males defend the territory. Incubation lasts approximately 13–15 days,{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=405}}{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1184}} starting with the last egg laid.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1184}} Occasionally, the male may feed the incubating female, but she also leaves the nest to forage independently. Usually both parents feed the chicks,{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=405}} though sometimes only females feed the chicks or do the majority of feeding. The female broods the chicks for about 5–6 days, spending all night and about half of the day doing so; after this period, she only broods at night.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1184}} Chicks fledge after around 19 days.{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=405}} They stay close to the nest in dense vegetation, where their parents continue to feed them. After about 3 days, the young birds begin to forage on their own, becoming fully independent in approximately another week or two.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1185}} On Tiritiri Matangi, nest success was 44 %, primarily hindered by predation and nest abandonment, contributing to the relatively low reproductive success. Bellbirds can begin breeding in their first year{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=405}} and can live at least eight years.{{Cite journal |last1=Spurr |first1=E. B. |last2=Crossland |first2=A. C. |last3=Sagar |first3=P. M. |date=2008 |title=Recapture of bellbird likely 8+ years old |url=https://www.birdsnz.org.nz/publications/recapture-of-bellbird-likely-8-years-old/ |journal=Notornis |volume=55 |issue=1 |page=45}} ===Diet=== Bellbirds consume [[nectar]], [[fruit]], [[Honeydew (secretion)|honeydew]], and [[insect]]s.{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=406}}{{Cite journal |last1=Spurr |first1=E. B. |last2=Rod |first2=S. |last3=Tranter |first3=K. P. |date=2011 |title=Food preferences of the bellbird (''Anthornis melanura'') in native forest remnants on the Port Hills, Banks Peninsula, New Zealand |url=https://www.birdsnz.org.nz/publications/food-preferences-of-the-bellbird-anthornis-melanura-in-native-forest-remnants-on-the-port-hills-banks-peninsula-new-zealand/ |journal=Notornis |volume=58 |issue=3&4 |pages=139–157 |ref=Spurr2011}} For most of the year, they feed on nectar from a large variety of native and, more recently, exotic plant species. However, in autumn, when nectar and honeydew are scarce, they eat more fruit.{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=406}}{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1178}} Females tend to consume more insects than males, likely because the more aggressive males exclude them from feeding territories.{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=406}} Fruit is typically consumed whole, with diameters mostly below 6 mm and occasionally up to 10 mm. [[File:Juvenile bellbird standing on a branch.jpg|left|thumb|Juvenile bellbird standing on a branch (Zealandia, Wellington)]] Nectar is taken from a variety of local plants, such as [[Fuchsia excorticata|New Zealand fuchsia]] (''Fuchsia excorticata''), [[kōwhai]] (''Sophora'' species), [[Neopanax arboreus|five-finger]] (''Neopanax arboreus''), [[Phormium|flax]] (''Phormium'' species), [[Metrosideros umbellata|southern rātā]] (''Metrosideros umbellata''), [[Melicytus ramiflorus|mahoe]] (''Melicytus ramiflorus''), [[Pseudowintera|horopito]] (''Pseudowintera'' species), [[Kunzea ericoides|kanuka]] (''Kunzea ericoides''), [[Schefflera digitata|pate]] (''Schefflera digitata''), [[Myrsine australis|red matipo]] (''Myrsine australis''), [[Ripogonum scandens|supplejack]] (''Ripogonum scandens''), [[Griselinia littoralis|broadleaf]] (''Griselinia littoralis''), [[Cordyline australis|cabbage tree]] (''Cordyline australis''), [[Plagianthus regius|ribbonwood]] (''Plagianthus regius'') and many others. Similar to nectar, fruit can be obtained from numerous species of trees and shrubs, such as karamū (''[[Coprosma lucida]], [[Coprosma robusta|C. robusta]]''), [[Aristotelia serrata|makomako]] (''Aristotelia serrata''), [[Dacrydium cupressinum|rimu]] (''Dacrydium cupressinum''), [[Dacrycarpus dacrydioides|kahikatea]] (''Dacrycarpus dacrydioides''), flax, red matipo or [[Neomyrtus pedunculata|rōhutu]] (''Neomyrtus pedunculata'').{{Cite journal |last1=Colin F. J. |first1=O'Donnell |last2=Dilks |first2=Peter J. |date=1994 |title=Foods and Foraging of Forest Birds in Temperate Rainforest, South Westland, New Zealand |url=https://newzealandecology.org/nzje/1953 |journal=New Zealand Journal of Ecology |volume=18 |issue=2 |pages=87–107}}{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|pp=1177–1179}} Favourite insects taken include [[Diptera|flies]] (Diptera), [[Hemiptera|true bugs]] (Hemiptera), and [[Cicadidae|true cicadas]] (Cicadidae).{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|pp=1179–1180}} Young birds primarily feed on insects such as [[caterpillar]]s, [[moth]]s, [[larva]]e, and [[spider]]s.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1180}} Bellbirds can gather food from all levels of the forest. They may also forage in flocks with other bird species, such as the [[Whitehead (bird)|whitehead]] (''Mohoua albicilla'').{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1177}} They obtain insects by gleaning them from all parts of a tree or by hawking.{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=406}} During the breeding season, they are territorial and forage alone. After breeding, they are typically solitary, even though multiple individuals may forage in the same tree simultaneously; each defends its own foraging territory within the tree.{{sfn|Higgins|Peter|Steele|2001|p=1177}} Bellbirds play an important part in [[Pollination|pollinating]] numerous native plants such as [[mistletoe]] (e.g. ''[[Peraxilla tetrapetala]]''), fuchsia, and kōwhai.[http://www.doc.govt.nz/conservation/native-animals/birds/land-birds/bellbird-korimako/facts/ Bellbird/korimako facts – Department of Conservation] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121005025617/http://www.doc.govt.nz/conservation/native-animals/birds/land-birds/bellbird-korimako/facts/ |date=5 October 2012 }} Retrieved: 13 February 2011{{Cite journal |last1=Murphy |first1=David J. |last2=Kelly |first2=Dave |date=2001 |title=Scarce or distracted ? Bellbird (''Anthornis melanura'') foraging and diet in an area inadequate mistletoe pollnation |url=https://newzealandecology.org/nzje/2119 |journal=New Zealand Journal of Ecology |volume=25 |issue=1 |pages=69–81 |ref=Murphy2001}} They are also important [[Seed dispersal|seed dispersers]]. ===Song and sound=== {{listen | filename = New Zealand Bellbirds (Anthornis melanura) singing on Tiritiri Matangi Island.ogg | title = Bellbirds singing | description = Several New Zealand bellbirds singing on [[Tiritiri Matangi Island]] | pos = right }} The New Zealand bellbirds have a distinctive vocal repertoire. Its pronounced singing voice was noted already by [[James Cook]] who thought that "it seemed to be like small bells most exquisitely tuned". Bellbird song varies regionally{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=172}} and is somewhat similar to the song of the tūī but it does not include harsh grunts, clicks and wheezes.{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=172}} Bellbirds utilise their song throughout the year to defend breeding territories and food resources.{{Cite journal |last1=Brunton |first1=Dianne H. |last2=Li |first2=Xiaoling |date=1 January 2006 |title=The song structure and seasonal patterns of vocal behaviour of male and female bellbirds (''Anthornis melanura'') |journal=Journal of Ethology |language=en |volume=24 |issue=1 |pages=17–25 |doi=10.1007/s10164-005-0155-5 |issn=1439-5444}} Bellbirds sing throughout the day but are especially active in the early morning and evening. The male's song features three distinct sounds reminiscent of chiming bells. The dawn song typically includes 2 to 6 pure, bell-like notes, sometimes interspersed with quieter tones, and can last between 10 and 40 minutes, particularly at dawn and during the breeding season. While singing, the male stretches his neck and fluffs his feathers. Singing males often join together in [[unison]]. Their full song includes a variety of sounds, such as clonks, chonks, quiet notes, and harsh noises. The female sings with quite pure frequency, producing songs of 7 to 15 notes. She can engage in countersinging with other females or perform duets with her mate. [[File:Anthornis melanura melanura 287804513.jpg|thumb|Female bellbird (Tiritiri Matangi)]] Song types differ significantly between the sexes, with males singing several structurally distinct types of songs, while females produce overlapping types in structure. Joint singing at dawn, as noted by Sir Joseph Banks in Queen Charlotte Sound during James Cook's first voyage to New Zealand, occurs primarily at dawn and dusk in areas with a high density of bellbirds and few other bird species. This phenomenon is only heard when many bellbirds are present at the same time. Their alarm call is a rapidly repeated set of harsh [[staccato]] "yeng". The modification of the ninth, sometimes also eight or tenth primaries allow bellbirds to make a specific whirring sound during flight. The wing sound frequencies can vary, usually staying below 1000 [[hertz]]. Bellbirds can control the production of these sounds and tūī, a major food competitor, can also make them. The wing whirling sounds are connected to the expression of aggression on feeding territories, territory defence but also courtship displays. == Conservation == === Historical decline === [[File:Walter Lawry Buller (English School).jpg|thumb|[[Walter Lawry Buller]] was one of the first ornithologists to mention a steep decline in bellbird numbers in the 1860s.]] Bellbirds were once widespread across New Zealand in high numbers. At the time of European arrival, they were one of the most common birds.{{sfn|Norman|2018|p=310}}{{sfn|Lee|2005|p=150}} Bellbird populations have declined significantly due to the destruction of native forests (initiated by Māori and perpetuated by Europeans{{Cite web |last=Dawson |first=John |date=2007 |title=Conifer–broadleaf forests – Loss of conifer–broadleaf forests |url= https://teara.govt.nz/en/interactive/11674/deforestation-of-new-zealand |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20240624012525/https://teara.govt.nz/en/interactive/11674/deforestation-of-new-zealand |archive-date=24 June 2024 |website=Te Ara – the Encyclopedia of New Zealand |access-date=24 June 2024 |url-status=live }}) and the introduction of invasive predators such as [[cat]]s (''Felis catus''), [[Mustelidae|mustelids]] including [[Mustela furo|ferret]]s (''Mustela furo''), [[stoat]]s (''M. erminea'') and [[weasel]]s (''M. nivalis''), and [[rodent]]s such as the [[brown rat]] (''Rattus norvegicus'') and the [[black rat]] (''Rattus rattus''). Between the 1860s and about 1900, bellbird numbers declined very dramatically, prompting many ornithologists to search for reasons beyond deforestation and introduced mammals.{{sfn|Norman|2018|p=310}}{{sfn|Lee|2005|p=}} [[Walter Buller]], the prominent New Zealand ornithologist of the 19th century, in 1871 quoted [[Hokianga]] resident [[Frederick Edward Maning|Frederick Maning]] saying:{{cite journal |last=Buller |first=Walter |date=May 1871 |title=Further notes on the ornithology of New Zealand |journal=Transactions and Proceedings of the New Zealand Institute, 1870 |volume=3 |article-number=9 |pages=45 |url= https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/periodicals/TPRSNZ1870-3.2.6.1.10 |access-date=7 May 2026}} {{Quotation|I remember the time, not very long ago, when the Maori lads would come out of the woods with hundreds of korimakos hung around them in strings, now one scarcely ever hears the bird; formerly they swarmed in the northern woods by thousands, now they are well nigh extinct.}} The decline started in the north of the country and moved south.{{sfn|Lee|2005|p=150}} By 1870, bellbirds had vanished from Northland, and between 1868 and 1878 they disappeared from [[Great Barrier Island]]. In the South Island, bellbird numbers in Canterbury did not reach their lowest point until 1900. While bellbirds declined across New Zealand's main islands, the impact was most pronounced in the North Island. After 1910, bellbird numbers in Canterbury started to surge. A comparable recovery, albeit earlier, occurred in many North Island areas, apart from northern areas such as Northland. [[J. G. Myers]] suggested that the decline could have been caused by a disease such as [[avian malaria]], which was introduced from Europe in birds of some non-native species.{{Cite journal |last=Myers |first=J. G. |author-link=J. G. Myers |date=1923 |title=The present position of the endemic birds of New Zealand |journal=New Zealand Journal of Science and Technology |volume=6 |pages=65–99}} This hypothesis is supported by the fact that offshore island populations were not as affected (the Great Barrier, with its highly modified habitat, is an exception), and bellbirds have survived in some mainland habitats with the same pressure of introduced mammals until the present day.{{sfn|Heather|Robertson|2015|p=405}} However, further evidence supporting a sudden spread of disease is lacking, and widespread sudden decrease in other [[Taxon|taxa]] suggest a wider ecological problem.{{sfn|Lee|2005|p=154}} [[File:Black Rat (Rattus rattus) under the Tree House (15858296885).jpg|left|thumb|The tree-climbing [[black rat]] is a frequent predator of bellbirds.]] The main cause of this sharp decline remains unclear. [[Andreas Reischek]] considered the decline to be caused by "cats, rats, bees and bush-fires."{{Cite journal |last=Reischek |first=Andreas |author-link= Andreas Reischek |date=1886 |title=Notes on Ornithology |url= http://rsnz.natlib.govt.nz/legalstatements.html |journal=Transactions and Proceedings of the Royal Society of New Zealand |volume=19 |pages=184–188}} Buller mentioned that some Māori from Northland believed that the decline was caused by an introduced [[honey bee]] that drove away the bellbirds from their flowers. Buller originally believed that the decline was caused by introduced [[rat]]s.{{sfn|Buller|1873|p=93}} However, in 1894 he cast doubts on this hypothesis after visiting [[Motutaiko Island]] in [[Lake Taupō]], infamous for its rat infestation, yet discovered a thriving bellbird population there.{{Cite journal |last=Buller |first=Walter |author-link=Walter Buller |date=1894 |title=Notes on the Ornithology of New Zealand; with an Exhibition of Rare Specimens |url= https://paperspast.natlib.govt.nz/periodicals/TPRSNZ1894-27.2.6.1.4 |journal= Transactions and Proceedings of the Royal Society of New Zealand |volume=27 |page=110}} Later authors suggested that rats still may have caused the decline of the bellbirds and that the rats Buller saw on Motutaiko Island were actually either brown rats{{sfn|Norman|2018|p=310}} or [[Polynesian rat]]s,{{cite journal |last=Turbott |first=E. G. |author-link=Graham Turbott |year=1953 |title=Notes on the occurrence of the Bellbird in North Auckland |url=https://www.birdsnz.org.nz/wp-content/uploads/2021/12/Notornis_5_6.pdf#page=4 |journal=Notornis |volume=5 |issue=6 |pages=175–178}} which do not climb trees frequently, and the sharp decline was caused by tree-climbing black rats.{{sfn|Norman|2018|p=310}} Black rats were introduced in northern New Zealand in the mid-19th century, and their rapid spread south indeed coincided with the decline of bellbirds (and some other [[passerine]]s, such as [[Philesturnus carunculatus|South Island saddleback]] (''Philesturnus carunculatus''), [[Cyanoramphus|parakeets]] (''Cyanoramphus'' species) and [[Bushwren|bush wren]] (''Xenicus longipes'')).{{sfn|Lee|2005|p=155}} Black rats are believed to be the primary reason for the extinction of the Chatham Island bellbird, which is another reason to suspect that they also played a significant role in the population decline of New Zealand bellbirds.{{Cite web |last1=Brown |first1=Kerry |last2=Elliott |first2=Graeme |last3=Innes |first3=John |last4=Kemp |first4=Josh |date=2015 |title=Ship rat, stoat and possum control on mainland New Zealand: An overview of techniques, successes and challenges |url=https://www.doc.govt.nz/Documents/conservation/threats-and-impacts/animal-pests/ship-rat-stoat-possum-control.pdf |publisher=New Zealand Department of Conservation |publication-place=Wellington}} Some other research also points to black rats{{Cite journal |last1=Innes |first1=John |last2=Kelly |first2=Dave |last3=Overton |first3=Jacob McC. |last4=Gillies |first4=Craig |date=2010 |title=Predation and other factors currently limiting New Zealand forest birds |url=https://newzealandecology.org/nzje/2911 |journal=New Zealand Journal of Ecology |volume=34 |issue=1 |pages=86–114}} or introduced mammalian predators in general.{{Cite journal |last1=Elliott |first1=Graeme P. |last2=Wilson |first2=Peter R. |last3=Taylor |first3=Rowley H. |last4=Beggs |first4=Jacqueline R. |date=1 September 2010 |title=Declines in common, widespread native birds in a mature temperate forest |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0006320710002594 |journal=Biological Conservation |volume=143 |issue=9 |pages=2119–2126 |doi=10.1016/j.biocon.2010.05.022 |bibcode=2010BCons.143.2119E |issn=0006-3207|url-access=subscription }} === Reintroductions === [[File:NZ Bellbird, tracking 01.jpg|thumb|Attaching tracking transmitter and antenna to a bellbird, [[Zealandia (wildlife sanctuary)|Zealandia wildlife sanctuary]], Wellington]] There have been many attempts to [[Species reintroduction|reintroduce]] bellbirds to areas of their former abundance. The first reintroduction occurred in 1932 when Auckland Zoological Society released 15 bellbirds from Little Barrier Island in the [[Waitākere Ranges]]. The bellbirds initially dispersed a few kilometres into the valley but gradually disappeared with no reports after 1946. During the second mainland release in March 1983 at Shakespear Regional Park, 22 birds were set free. Only one nest was reported, and soon after, bellbirds disappeared, with some observed returning to their home island of Tiritiri Matangi. Similar outcomes were seen in a few other release attempts, where bellbirds vanished shortly after being released.{{sfn|Lee|2005|p=151}}{{Cite journal |last1=Miskelly |first1=Colin M. |last2=Powlesland |first2=Ralph G. |date=2013 |title=Conservation translocations of New Zealand birds, 1863–2012 |url=https://www.birdsnz.org.nz/publications/conservation-translocations-of-new-zealand-birds-1863-2012/ |journal=Notornis |volume=60 |issue=1 |pages=3–28}} As of 2012, a few more reintroductions were in progress. The cause of failures of reintroductions of bellbirds is often difficult to determine, but predation by mammalian predators, mainly black rats, is thought to be a critical factor.{{sfn|Lee|2005|p=156}} There is a potential possibility to [[Species translocation|translocate]] bellbirds to [[Chatham Islands]] in order to replace the [[ecological niche]] of their extinct relative, the Chatham bellbird.{{Cite book |last1=Aikman |first1=Hilary |url=https://www.doc.govt.nz/globalassets/documents/science-and-technical/birds_of_the_chatham_islands.pdf |title=Birds of the Chatham Islands |last2=Miskelly |first2=Colin |publisher=Department of Conservation |year=2004 |isbn=0-478-22565-2 |location=Wellington |pages=10–11}} [[File:Mustela Erminea head.jpg|left|thumb|The [[stoat]], a predator of bellbirds]] === Threats and population assessment === Despite the bellbirds' widespread presence on the New Zealand mainland, their populations remain low due to the impact of mammalian predators like rats and stoats. Invasive mammalian predators are the main threats to bellbirds. In beech forest the main predator is the stoat, while in non-beech forest areas the main predator is likely the black rat. In areas of the mainland where bellbirds are found, their numbers are typically lower compared to predator-free islands. Although possums have not been shown to directly kill bellbirds, they compete with them for food resources. Introduced social [[wasp]]s like [[Vespula germanica|German wasps]] (''Vespula germanica''), and particularly [[Vespula vulgaris|common wasps]] (''V. vulgaris''), have been found to significantly diminish the availability of honeydew drops, which are an important food source for bellbirds.{{Cite journal |last1=Moller |first1=H. |last2=Tilley |first2=J. A. V. |last3=Thomas |first3=B. W. |last4=Gaze |first4=P. D. |date=1991 |title=Effect of introduced social wasps on the standing crop of honeydew in New Zealand beech forests |url=http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/03014223.1991.10757964 |journal=New Zealand Journal of Zoology |language=en |volume=18 |issue=2 |pages=171–179 |doi=10.1080/03014223.1991.10757964 |issn=0301-4223|url-access=subscription }}{{Cite journal |last1=Gardner-Gee |first1=Robin |last2=Beggs |first2=Jacqueline R. |date=2013 |title=Invasive wasps, not birds, dominate in a temperate honeydew system |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1442-9993.2012.02412.x |journal=Austral Ecology |language=en |volume=38 |issue=3 |pages=346–354 |doi=10.1111/j.1442-9993.2012.02412.x |bibcode=2013AusEc..38..346G |issn=1442-9985|url-access=subscription }}{{Cite journal |last1=Lester |first1=Phil |last2=Beggs |first2=Jacqueline |last3=Brown |first3=Bob |last4=Edwards |first4=Eric |last5=Groenteman |first5=Ronny |last6=Toft |first6=Richard |last7=Twidle |first7=Andrew |last8=Ward |first8=Darren |date=2013 |title=The outlook for control of New Zealand's most abundant, widespread and damaging invertebrate pests: social wasps |url=https://ojs.victoria.ac.nz/nzsr/article/view/8698 |journal=New Zealand Science Review |language=en |volume=70 |issue=4 |pages=56–62 |doi=10.26686/nzsr.v70.8698 |issn=2624-277X|doi-access=free }} The [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] (IUCN) classifies bellbirds as a species of [[Least-concern species|least concern]]. New Zealand [[Department of Conservation (New Zealand)|Department of Conservation]] considers the subspecies ''melanura'' as "not-threatened", while the island subspecies ''obscura'' and ''oneho'' are "naturally uncommon".{{Cite web |last=Robertson |first=Hugh A. |display-authors=et al. |date=2021 |title=Conservation status of birds in Aotearoa New Zealand, 2021 |url=https://www.doc.govt.nz/globalassets/documents/science-and-technical/nztcs36entire.pdf |website=Department of Conservation Te Papa Atawhai}} According to IUCN and a 2010 study, the overall bellbird population is suspected to be in decline as a result of ongoing [[habitat destruction]] and predation by introduced mammals.{{Cite journal |date=2017 |title=Anthornis melanura |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22704447/110985953 |website=The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2017-1.RLTS.T22704447A110985953.en}} The decline is most prominent in lowland areas under 1000 metres in elevation. A 2022 study of New Zealand garden birds concluded that bellbird numbers are stable or locally increasing.{{Cite journal |last1=MacLeod |first1=Catriona J. |last2=Green |first2=Peter |last3=Howard |first3=Simon |last4=Gormley |first4=Andrew M. |last5=Brandt |first5=Angela J. |last6=Spurr |first6=Eric B. |date=2022 |title=Assessing the state of New Zealand's garden birds from national to local scales |journal=Ecological Solutions and Evidence |language=en |volume=3 |issue=1 |doi=10.1002/2688-8319.12121 |issn=2688-8319|doi-access=free |bibcode=2022EcoSE...3E2121M }} Known increases of bellbirds in some areas like Christchurch [[Port Hills]], [[Lake Rotoiti (Tasman)|Lake Rotoiti]], [[Craigieburn Forest Park|Craigieburns]],{{Cite journal |last1=Kelly |first1=Dave |last2=Brindle |first2=Chelsea |last3=Ladley |first3=Jenny J. |last4=Robertson |first4=Alastair W. |last5=Maddigan |first5=Fraser W. |last6=Butler |first6=Jeff |last7=Ward-Smith |first7=Tamsin |last8=Murphy |first8=David J. |last9=Sessions |first9=Laura A. |date=2005 |title=Can stoat (''Mustela erminea'') trapping increase bellbird (''Anthornis melanura'') populations and benefit mistletoe (''Peraxilla tetrapetala'') pollination? |url=https://newzealandecology.org/nzje/2249 |journal=New Zealand Journal of Ecology |volume=29 |issue=1 |pages=69–82 |issn=0110-6465}} [[Kapiti Island]] or [[Tiritiri Matangi Island|Tiritiri Matangi]] are related to the control of invasive mammalian species.{{Cite journal |last1=Spurr |first1=E. B. |last2=Crossland |first2=A. C. |last3=Sagar |first3=P. M. |date=2014 |title=Increased abundance of the bellbird (''Anthornis melanura'') in Christchurch, New Zealand |url=https://www.birdsnz.org.nz/wp-content/uploads/2021/12/Spurr_et_al._2014.pdf |journal=Notornis |volume=61 |issue=2 |pages=67–74}} == Relationship with humans == === In Māori culture === [[File:Bul01Bird089a.jpg|thumb|A [[Snare trap|snare]] called ''{{lang|mi|tuke}}'' was used by Māori to catch bellbirds.]] The bellbird is regarded as ''[[taonga]]'' by the [[Māori people|Māori]], who traditionally valued it for its meat and singing abilities. There are many {{lang|mi|whakataukī}} ([[proverb]]s) that refer to the bellbird's singing. Early Māori, who deeply appreciated songs and [[oratories]], compared skilled performers to bellbirds by saying "{{lang|mi|He rite ki te kōpara e kō nei i te ata}}" (''Just like a korimako singing at dawn'').{{sfn|Orbell|2003|p=70}} In addition to proverbs highlighting the bellbird's singing abilities, some compare the restless nature of female bellbirds, who are constantly on the move, to that of irresponsible or even promiscuous women. An example is "{{lang|mi|Ehara! He kōpara kai rerere.}}" (''Hey! The bellbird is a restless bird.'') which could describe an irresponsible woman.{{sfn|Riley|2001|p=50}} A well-known Māori song goes as follows:{{sfn|Orbell|2003|p=71}} {{Verse translation|lang1=mi|italicsoff=y| Hūtia te rito o te harakeke, kei hea te kōmako e kō? Kī mai ki a au, 'He aha te mea nui i te ao?' Māku e kī atu, 'He tāngata, he tāngata, he tāngata. |attr1= |If the flax heart is pulled out Where will the korimako [bellbird] sing? Ask me, 'What is most important in this world?' And I will reply, 'People, people, people'}} In the song above, the korimako (bellbird) represents humanity, while the [[New Zealand flax|flax]] symbolises the vitality of the land and its people. The proverb signifies that individuals will thrive if they receive the right support.{{sfn|Orbell|2003|p=71}} New Zealand missionary [[Richard Taylor (missionary)|Richard Taylor]] wrote in 1855 that in the [[Taupō|Taupō region]] during a child's naming ceremony, a bellbird was cooked in a small, specially dedicated oven. Shortly after cooking, this "sweetest singing bird of New Zealand" was ritually eaten by a chief priest (''{{lang|mi|tino ariki}}'') to ensure that "the child might have a sweet voice, and become an admired orator".{{sfn|Orbell|2003|pp=70–71}}{{Cite book |last=Taylor |first=Richard |author-link=Richard Taylor (missionary) |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0gJMAAAAYAAJ |title=Te Ika a Maui, Or, New Zealand and Its Inhabitants |publisher=Wertheim and Macintosh |year=1855 |location=London |pages=74–75}} Such customs varied across the country. In some birth ceremonies, the bellbird was released alive rather than being cooked and eaten. Occasionally, a young chief was given bellbird meat as he grew to adulthood, with the hope that he would become a successful orator. A well-spoken orator was often simply called a ''{{lang|mi|korimako}}'' (bellbird).{{sfn|Riley|2001|p=50}} Bellbirds often eat the fruit of a small forest tree, ''[[Pennantia corymbosa]]'', hence its Māori name ''{{lang|mi|kaikōmako}}'' ("food of the bellbird").{{sfn|Riley|2001|p=53}} The Māori hunted bellbirds for their meat using various methods. They would employ [[spear]]s or long, thin rods to strike the birds. Ground [[snare trap]]s, known as ''{{lang|mi|puaka}}'', were baited with honey flowers such as those from the [[pōhutukawa]] tree. During direct hunting, a fowler would hide in a shelter made from vegetation such as fern [[frond]]s or [[Rhopalostylis sapida|nīkau palm]] leaves. The fowler would then set up a low-lying horizontal perch snare above the shelter and attract bellbirds by imitating their calls, using a leaf held between the lips. Once a bird landed on the perch, the fowler would strike it.{{sfn|Riley|2001|p=52}} A method of catching live bellbirds involved using a trap called a ''{{lang|mi|tuke}}'', which was a long, thin rod ending with a short horizontal section and a loop at the end of a long string. The trap's design allowed the trapper to close the loop when a bird landed on the end of the ''{{lang|mi|tuke}}'', catching its feet.{{sfn|Buller|1873|pp=89–90}} Preparing a bellbird meal involved cooking the entire birds in earth ovens ([[hāngī]]). The bellbirds were plucked, but their internal organs were left intact to infuse a special flavour. Another cooking method involved wrapping the bellbirds in large [[Meryta sinclairii|puka]] or [[Brachyglottis repanda|rangiora]] leaves and placing them in a shallow hole, about 15 cm deep. They were then covered with ashes and earth until cooked, ensuring that the juices, fat, and aromas were sealed in.{{sfn|Riley|2001|p=52}} === In culture === The call of ''Anthornis melanura'' is used by [[Radio New Zealand]] as an [[interval signal]].{{Cite web |date=5 February 2014 |title=Listen to RNZI's identification signal – the bellbird |url=https://www.rnz.co.nz/national/programmes/birds-on-morning-report/audio/2584672/radio-new-zealand-international-s-bellbird |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240624075049/https://www.rnz.co.nz/national/programmes/birds-on-morning-report/audio/2584672/radio-new-zealand-international-s-bellbird |archive-date=24 June 2024 |access-date=24 June 2024 |website=[[RNZ]] |url-status=live }} The movie ''[[Bellbird (film)|Bellbird]]'' was named after the New Zealand bellbird, as are places like Lake Bellbird{{Cite web |title=Lake Bellbird, West Coast |url=https://www.topomap.co.nz/NZTopoMap/nz39927/Lake-Bellbird/ |access-date=24 June 2024 |website=NZ Topo Map |language=en}} on the [[West Coast Region|West Coast]] and the historic building site the Sign of the Bellbird in the [[Port Hills]] near Christchurch.{{Cite web |title=Crater Rim – Sign of the Kiwi to Sign of the Bellbird |url=https://ccc.govt.nz/parks-and-gardens/explore-parks/port-hills/kennedys-bush/crater-rim-sign-of-the-kiwi-to-sign-of-the-bellbird |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240624075521/https://ccc.govt.nz/parks-and-gardens/explore-parks/port-hills/kennedys-bush/crater-rim-sign-of-the-kiwi-to-sign-of-the-bellbird |archive-date=24 June 2024 |access-date=24 June 2024 |website=Christchurch City Council |url-status=live}} ==References== {{Reflist}} === Works cited === *{{cite book |last1=Best |first1=Elsdon |title=Forest lore of the Māori: with methods of snaring, trapping, and preserving birds and rats, uses of berries, roots, fern-root, and forest products, with mythological notes on origins, karakia used etc. |date=1977 |publisher=E.
